transcript.presenting_concern: Test anxiety and perfectionism — cognitive restructuring with teen

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Hey Ethan, good to see you. How was your week? I know you had that history test on Thursday — I've been curious how it went.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The test was okay actually. Like, better than I expected. I didn't freak out as bad as last time. I used the breathing thing before it started — the 4-7-8 one — and it helped a little. At least enough that I could read the first question without my vision going blurry.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Nice! Tell me more about that. Walk me through what the experience was like compared to last time.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So last time — the chemistry test two weeks ago — I blanked on the first question and then my brain went into full panic mode. Like, "you're going to fail, everyone else is already on question three, you're the dumbest person in this room, you shouldn't even be in honors." Full spiral. I couldn't write anything for like five minutes. This time, I blanked on a question too — question four, something about the Congress of Vienna — but instead of spiraling I told myself "skip it, come back to it, it's just one question" and moved on. When I came back to it at the end, I actually remembered the answer. It was like my brain needed time without pressure.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a huge shift, Ethan. Really impressive. Last time the blank triggered catastrophizing and you got paralyzed. This time you recognized what was happening, used a strategy — skipping and returning — and it worked. You adapted in real time. What made you able to do that differently?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I think it was the thought catching we practiced. Like, I noticed the "you're stupid" thought starting to form — like I could feel it coming — and I was like "oh, there it is. That's the anxiety talking, not facts." It still FELT true in the moment but I could kind of... separate from it for a second. Long enough to skip the question and keep moving.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That separation is called cognitive defusion — noticing the thought without buying into it completely. "I'm having the thought that I'm stupid" is very different from "I AM stupid." One is an observation — you watching your own brain — and the other is an identity statement. You're getting much better at that observer position. How did it feel afterward?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Good. I was actually kind of proud? Like, I proved I could handle it. I ended up getting an 88 on the test, which isn't my best but for a test where I blanked on a question and didn't spiral, I'll take it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: An 88 while managing anxiety in real time! That's a win. And I want to notice something — you said "isn't my best" and then immediately reframed to "I'll take it." That's growth. Three weeks ago, an 88 would have felt like failure to you.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah... I mean, a part of me still wishes it was a 95. But I'm trying to let that go.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: We'll keep working on that. The perfectionism doesn't disappear overnight — it loosens gradually. An 88 on a hard test while managing an anxiety episode is genuinely excellent. So that was the history test. But I'm guessing the week wasn't all wins. What else happened?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. The SAT prep test on Saturday — I was a mess. Totally different story. Couldn't focus, kept checking the clock, my heart was racing the whole time, and my score dropped twenty points from my last practice test. Went from 1380 to 1360. Which I know isn't a huge drop but it felt like everything was going backward.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay, a couple things. First — 1380 to 1360 is within normal score variation. That's not a meaningful decline. But I hear that it FELT significant to you. What was different about the SAT prep experience versus the history test?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The stakes feel so much higher. The history test is just one grade in one class. If I get an 88, whatever, my grade drops to an A-minus. The SAT is like... my future. Whether I get into a good school. Whether my parents think I'm smart enough. Whether I'm going to be successful or not. It feels like one number is going to decide my whole life.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: There's a big belief packed into that — "one number decides my whole life." Let's put a pin in it because we're definitely going to work with it. But first — you mentioned your parents. Tell me what the pressure from them looks like specifically.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So my sister Maya got a 1520. She's at Stanford now. And ever since she got in, it's like the expectation is that I'll do the same thing — get a 1500-plus, get into a top school. My dad has this spreadsheet of colleges with their average SAT scores and he shows it to me like it's a battle plan. "These are our targets, here's where you need to be." He doesn't ask what schools I'M interested in — he tells me where I SHOULD want to go.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What would happen if you told your dad you don't want to go to Stanford? Or that a 1360 might be your score?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Disappointment. That look he gets — he doesn't yell, he just gets quiet and looks away, like I'm not worth engaging with. That's almost worse than yelling. My mom tries to soften it — she'll say "we just want the best for you, honey" — but what they mean by "the best" is "what Maya did." I'm not Maya. I don't WANT to be Maya. But they don't see me as a separate person with different strengths.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That sounds really frustrating and lonely — feeling like you're being measured against a template that doesn't fit you. And the way your dad disengages when he's disappointed — that withdrawal — sounds painful. Like you have to earn his attention through performance.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet] Yeah. Pretty much. If I bring home a good grade, he's engaged, he's proud, he tells people. If I don't... he just goes back to his phone. It's like I stop existing if I'm not excelling.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Has it always been that way?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Since middle school, when grades started "mattering." When I was little it was different — he'd play with me, take me to baseball games. But once school became competitive, it shifted. Everything became about performance. He tracks my grades online — he checks the parent portal before I even see my scores sometimes. He'll text me "what happened on the Spanish quiz?" before I even get out of class.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That level of monitoring — how does it make you feel?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Like I have no space. Like there's nowhere to just be average or mess up without it being a big deal. Every assignment feels like it's being watched. Even this class I love — creative writing — he doesn't care about because it "doesn't count for anything." But it's the one place I feel relaxed because there's no rubric. You just write what you feel and Mrs. Patterson says encouraging things.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So creative writing feels safe because there's no score, no comparison, no parental surveillance. That tells us something important: your anxiety is heavily contextual. In environments without evaluative pressure, you thrive. In environments with high stakes and parental expectations, the anxiety escalates. It's not that YOUR BRAIN is broken — it's that certain environments activate your threat system.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Huh. I never thought of it like that. It's not ALL the time — it's specific situations.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. Anxiety isn't a character trait — it's a response pattern. And we can change the response. Okay, let's do some cognitive restructuring on the big belief we identified: "The SAT score decides my whole life." We did this with the history test catastrophe and it helped, so let's try the same process with the SAT. Ready?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Hit me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: First, let's name the automatic thought clearly. What's the exact sentence your anxiety says about the SAT?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "If I don't get at least a 1450, I won't get into a good school, my parents will be disappointed, and my future is ruined."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. That's actually three linked beliefs: one, I need a 1450 or higher; two, anything less means bad schools; three, bad school means ruined future. Let's examine each one. First — where does the 1450 number come from?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My dad's spreadsheet. He says anything under 1450 takes the top-tier schools off the table.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Is that accurate?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I mean... sort of? Some top schools have averages around there. But plenty of people get into great schools with lower scores. SAT is just one factor — there's GPA, essays, activities, recommendations. My English teacher said my writing could be a real strength in applications.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So even by objective standards, the SAT is one factor among many. It's not a single pass/fail gate. What's your current GPA?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: 3.85 unweighted. 4.2 weighted with honors and AP classes.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And extracurriculars?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Varsity basketball, creative writing club president, I volunteer at the animal shelter on weekends, and I'm doing a summer internship at the newspaper.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Ethan. You have a 4.2 weighted GPA, you're a varsity athlete, you lead a club, you volunteer, and you'll have a journalism internship. Do you know what admissions officers actually look at?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I guess... the whole picture?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The whole picture. And your whole picture is strong. The SAT is a data point within a strong application — not the whole application. Your anxiety is telling you it's everything. The data says it's one piece. Now, second belief — "anything less means bad schools." What defines a "bad school"?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I don't actually think any school is bad. It's more like... not impressive enough. Not a name people recognize. My dad went to a state school and he's always talking about how he wishes he'd gone somewhere better. Like it held him back.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: But did it hold him back? What does your dad do?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: He's a director of engineering at a medical device company. He makes good money. He has a nice life. He just... feels like he could have done MORE if he'd gone to a better school.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So he's projecting his own regret onto you. He's trying to give you the opportunity he feels he missed. That's coming from love, in a way — but it's HIS wound, not yours. The irony is that he went to a state school and became a director of engineering. So the evidence of his own life contradicts the belief that school prestige determines success. Your dad is living proof that it doesn't.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [laughs] Oh man. I never thought about it that way. He's literally the counter-example to his own argument.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And third belief — "my future is ruined." Let's reality-test this one. Can you think of anyone successful who didn't go to an elite school? Beyond your dad.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I mean... tons of people. My uncle went to community college and owns three restaurants. My basketball coach went to a state school and he's doing what he loves. Steve Jobs dropped out entirely. I know those are extreme examples but...

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: They're not extreme — they're data points. Success is not linearly correlated with school prestige. Research actually shows that for most students, it's what you DO at college — how you engage, what connections you make, what skills you develop — that predicts success, not the name on the diploma. The belief that one test score ruins your future is catastrophizing — jumping to the worst possible conclusion with no evidence to support it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I KNOW all this logically. I really do. But when I'm sitting in that test room with the timer running, all the logic disappears and it's just pure fear.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's because anxiety operates in the emotional brain — the amygdala — which is faster than the logical brain. The rational parts of your brain know you're fine. But the emotional system screams "DANGER!" and it's louder. That's why we practice these restructuring exercises — repetition helps the rational brain speak louder over time. It's like building a muscle. Each time you catch the catastrophic thought and counter it with evidence, the neural pathway for the rational response gets stronger. You did it on the history test. Eventually, you'll do it on the SAT.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: How long does it take?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It varies. But you're already showing progress after five sessions. The history test was evidence that it's working. The SAT practice test shows us where we still need to build skills. Let's talk about what to do in high-stakes moments when the logic disappears. You said you used the breathing before the history test and it helped. What about during the SAT prep?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I didn't use it. I was too panicked to remember. By the time I thought "I should breathe," I was already deep in the spiral.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So we need a way to catch it earlier — before you're deep in the spiral. Let's talk about early warning signs. What's the FIRST thing you notice when test anxiety starts? Not the full panic — the very first signal.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [thinking] My hands get cold. Like, weirdly cold and slightly sweaty at the same time. And my leg starts bouncing. That happens before the thoughts start racing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Perfect. Cold hands and leg bouncing are your early warning system. They're your body's way of saying "anxiety is activating." If you can notice those physical cues and intervene THEN — before the cognitive spiral starts — you have a much better chance of staying in the rational zone. So the sequence would be: notice cold hands → that's my cue → do three deep breaths → remind myself of one rational thought → continue the test. Let's practice that now. Close your eyes.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [closes eyes] Okay.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Imagine you're sitting down to take the SAT. The proctor has said "begin." You open the booklet. You read the first question and you're not immediately sure of the answer. What do you notice in your body?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] Hands are getting cold. Stomach is tight. Leg wants to bounce.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good — you're catching it. Now what?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I notice it. "My hands are cold — that's the anxiety signal." And I do... [breathes in slowly, holds, exhales] The breathing. 4-7-8.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. One more breath. Now — what's the rational thought?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "My practice scores say I can do this. Skip and come back. One question doesn't decide everything."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Open your eyes. How was that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Easier than I thought. In my imagination it worked. The real test is whether I can do it when it's actually happening.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. And the more we rehearse it here — in a calm environment — the more automatic it becomes in a stressful one. It's like basketball. You don't learn free throws during the championship game. You practice them in empty gyms so that when the pressure is on, your body knows what to do. This is mental free-throw practice.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [grins] I like that analogy.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Now let's talk about the coping card from last time. Did you make one?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah! I wrote my average practice score — 1360 — the target school range — 1340 to 1380 — and "Anxiety is not facts. My scores say I can do this."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Perfect. And will you be allowed to look at it before the actual SAT?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Not during the test, but I can read it in the waiting area before we go in. Or in the car on the way there.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Read it in the car and once more in the waiting area. Let it be the last thing you see before you walk in. Now — let's talk about the family piece a bit more because I think it's fueling a lot of this. Last session you mentioned your parents compare you to Maya. Has anything new come up this week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah, actually. So at dinner on Sunday, my dad asked Maya — she was home for the weekend — to "give Ethan some SAT tips." Like, openly, at the dinner table. And Maya was cool about it, she just said "I honestly don't remember much, just do lots of practice tests." But it was mortifying. Like he's putting my sister on a pedestal in front of me and saying "be like her." I wanted to leave the table.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What did you do?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I just sat there and ate my chicken. Didn't say anything. Went to my room after. Put on headphones and played guitar for like an hour until I calmed down.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Playing guitar was a good coping choice — it's a form of emotional regulation through physical and creative engagement. But I also notice you went silent and withdrew. What would you have WANTED to say if you could have said anything without consequences?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] "I'm not Maya. Stop treating me like a worse version of her. I have my own strengths and my own path and I'm sick of being compared to someone I'm never going to be." Something like that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How did it feel to say that just now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Scary. But also kind of... good?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You have a right to that feeling. You have a right to be seen as yourself, not as Maya 2.0. We talked last time about potentially having your mom come to a session. Have you thought more about that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I mentioned it to my mom actually. She said she'd come. She's more... she gets it more than Dad does. She knows the comparisons bother me because she's seen me get upset. She just doesn't know how to stop Dad from doing it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Would it be helpful for me to talk with your mom about how pressure and comparison specifically worsen anxiety? Sometimes parents don't realize that their "motivation" is actually undermining performance. The research is clear — external pressure beyond a moderate level decreases test performance. Your dad's approach is likely making your scores WORSE, not better.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. If she could hear that from a professional, maybe she'd push back on Dad a little. Or at least stop the Maya comparisons at dinner.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's plan for that. I'll reach out to your mom this week and schedule a parent session — maybe the following week. It won't be about blaming your parents. It'll be about education: here's how anxiety works in adolescents, here's what helps, here's what hurts. Sound good?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Just... don't tell them everything I say here, right?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. What you share here is confidential unless there's a safety concern. I'll share general information about anxiety and strategies — not your private thoughts and feelings. You control what they know about your inner world.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. Good. Thanks.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Now — let's talk about perfectionism more broadly, because I think it's the engine underneath both the test anxiety and the family dynamics. You've internalized the message that your worth is tied to your performance — that you have to be excellent to be valued. When did that start?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Probably middle school, like I said. When grades started counting for high school placement. My parents started paying attention to every quiz, every assignment. Maya was already in all honors classes by then so the expectation was clear — I was supposed to follow her path. And I did, mostly. But she made it look easy. For me it takes effort. I study way more than she ever did.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So you're working harder for similar results, and that effort is invisible to your parents because they only see the outcome.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Exactly! They don't see the three hours I spend studying for a test. They just see the grade. And if it's not an A, they assume I wasn't trying hard enough. But I AM trying. I'm trying SO hard. Sometimes I feel like I can't try any harder and it still won't be enough.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That belief — "no matter how hard I try, it won't be enough" — is painful. And it's a perfectionism trap. Because perfectionism doesn't have a finish line. You get a 95, it should have been 100. You get 100, it should have been faster. You get into a great school, it should have been a better one. There's always a higher bar. The goalpost moves constantly.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. I've never thought about it stopping. Like, even if I get a 1500 on the SAT, then it's "get into Stanford," then it's "get a 4.0 there," then it's "get into the best grad school." When does it end?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Great question. For some people it never ends — they achieve and achieve and never feel good enough. That's the trajectory perfectionism puts you on. But there's another option: redefining success. Instead of "success = meeting external standards," what if success meant something else for you? What would YOUR definition look like, if your parents' voices weren't in the equation?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] I think... success would be doing work I care about. Like writing. Or maybe journalism. And having time for basketball and friends and music. Not being stressed all the time. Being able to enjoy things without this constant pressure that I should be studying instead.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That sounds like a rich, meaningful life. And notice — none of what you said requires a 1520 SAT score.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [quiet laugh] Yeah. Huh. I guess it doesn't.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: I want to do one more exercise before we wrap up. Let's practice some self-compassion, because perfectionism and self-compassion are opposites. Imagine your best friend — let's say your friend Marcus — comes to you and says "I got a 1360 on my SAT practice test and I'm freaking out." What would you say to him?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'd say "dude, 1360 is a great score. That's above average. You'll be fine. Stop stressing."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Would you call him stupid? Tell him his future is ruined?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No way. That would be terrible.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So you'd treat Marcus with kindness and rational perspective. Now — can you offer yourself the same kindness? Can you say "Ethan, 1360 is a good score. You'll be fine. Stop stressing"?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] It's so much harder to say it to myself. It feels like lying when I say it to me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the double standard of perfectionism. Other people deserve compassion. You don't. Other people's 1360 is great. Yours is failure. See the pattern?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. That's messed up. I'd never treat a friend the way I treat myself.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. So here's a new practice for you this week: when you catch yourself in self-criticism — "you're not good enough, you should be better, you're stupid" — I want you to pause and ask one question: "Would I say this to Marcus?" If the answer is no, you don't get to say it to yourself either.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The Marcus test. I like that. Simple enough to remember.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Simple is good. Okay, let's review your homework for this week. One: continue the 4-7-8 breathing before any test or stressful situation. Two: keep the coping card with you — read it before high-pressure moments. Three: practice the early warning system — when you notice cold hands and leg bouncing, that's your cue to intervene with breathing and one rational thought. Four: the "Marcus test" for self-criticism. And five — from last session — write down three things you're good at that have nothing to do with test scores. Did you do that last week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Oh yeah! I forgot to tell you. I wrote: writing — my English teacher said my last essay was "publishable quality." Basketball — I'm starting point guard. And being a good listener — my friends always come to me with their problems.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Those are real, meaningful, impressive strengths. A published-quality writer, a starting varsity athlete, a trusted friend. None of those show up on a standardized test. They show up in life. Keep that list somewhere visible — maybe in your phone, maybe taped inside your planner. Look at it when the perfectionism voice tells you you're not good enough.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'll put it as my phone background or something.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Love it. Alright, two more things. I'm going to reach out to your mom about a parent education session — probably the week after next. And one last thought for you to sit with: you told me your dad's definition of success — top SAT, top school, impressive career. You also told me YOUR definition — meaningful work, creativity, balance, not being stressed all the time. Those are two different paths. Eventually, you'll need to choose which one you walk. Not today. But keep noticing the difference between his voice and yours.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That's a lot to think about.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It is. And you have time. You're sixteen — you don't have to have it all figured out. That's another thing anxiety tells you: "decide now, or it's too late." The truth is you have years to figure out who you are and what you want. The urgency is artificial.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Thanks, Dr. Huang. I feel less panicked about the SAT right now than I have all week.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. And Ethan — that history test? The fact that you caught the spiral and redirected in real time? That shows me your brain is absolutely capable of managing anxiety. We just need to extend that skill to the higher-stakes situations. You built the muscle on a smaller weight. Now we're gradually increasing the load. You've got this.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Same time next week?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Same time. Keep practicing. And remember — you are more than a number.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: You are more than a number. That should be on a bumper sticker.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [laughs] Maybe I'll make one. See you next week.

goals: Test anxiety and perfectionism — cognitive restructuring with teen
